definition: Liquidity resting at swing highs (buy-side) and swing lows (sell-side) outside the current price range, where stop orders accumulate. Price sweeps these levels, triggering orders, then often reverses back into the range. This manipulation phase precedes the true directional move, occurring characteristically during high-probability session windows.
identification_rules: Mark the current price range by identifying its swing high (Range High) and swing low (Range Low) on the working time frame.

Identify resting liquidity beyond those boundaries: buy-side liquidity above the Range High (equal highs, prior swing highs, Previous Day High) and sell-side liquidity below the Range Low (equal lows, prior swing lows, Previous Day Low).

Confirm an external liquidity sweep when price extends beyond the Range High or Range Low, triggers those orders, and then closes back inside the range rather than sustaining the breakout on a higher time frame.

Note directional context: a sweep of external liquidity on the opposite side of the prevailing order flow (e.g., sweeping sell-side liquidity in a bullish context) signals manipulation before continuation.
Look for the sweep to occur during a high-probability session window (London 02:00–05:00 or New York AM 08:30–09:30 EST) to confirm it is a session-driven manipulation move.

Mark swing highs and swing lows (including session/range extremes such as Asia or London range) as external range liquidity; swing highs represent buy-side liquidity (resting buy stops), swing lows represent sell-side liquidity (resting sell stops).
A stop raid (sweep) in the opposite direction often precedes a move to external liquidity.

If price sweeps an external level but fails to displace or break out, treat it as a liquidity grab returning into the range (especially in Seek and Destroy conditions where both sides may be swept without follow-through).
After price interacts with internal range liquidity (e.g., a fair value gap) and respects it, the next target is the opposing external range liquidity (old high or old low).
After price takes out external range liquidity, expect price to seek internal range liquidity (the nearest fair value gap) before continuing.
External range liquidity levels are the most recent swing highs/lows beyond the current consolidation.
Apply these rules on any timeframe (fractal: weekly, daily, hourly, 5-minute, 1-minute).
In the current price range, find a single prominent Swing High or Swing Low (with lower highs on both sides or higher lows on both sides) that has not yet been touched or broken.
Distinguish from Relatively Equal Highs/Lows: external liquidity is a single isolated swing point, not two or more clustered at similar prices.
Specific levels to mark include: Previous Week High/Low, Previous Day High/Low, Session High/Low (Asia, London, New York).
When price has already filled Internal Range Liquidity (Fair Value Gap), mark the nearest untouched Swing High or Swing Low as the next External Range Liquidity target.

Determine if external liquidity has been swept: price pierces the high/low but closes back inside the range → Liquidity Sweep complete; if price closes outside, it is not considered a sweep with reversal.
trading_implication: External Range Liquidity provides directional bias and profit targets. After price respects internal liquidity (fair value gaps), continuation to the opposing external liquidity is expected, framing buy/sell models. Higher timeframes set weekly/daily bias; lower timeframes provide entry targets. Stops are placed beyond swept external levels, and runners can be held to higher-timeframe external targets. In non-displacement sweeps (e.g., Seek and Destroy conditions), traders fade the sweep back into the range targeting equilibrium or the opposite side.
common_mistakes: Treating the external liquidity sweep as a genuine breakout and entering in the direction of the sweep, rather than waiting for price to reverse back into the range.
Ignoring the return-to-range dynamic and holding a position in the direction of the sweep instead of anticipating reversion to internal liquidity targets.

Failing to distinguish external liquidity (beyond swing highs/lows) from internal liquidity (fair value gaps or equal highs/lows inside the range), leading to premature entries before the sweep is complete.
Expecting a clean retracement to internal liquidity before every move to external — price may continue without retracing.
Attempting to project the next leg before a fair value gap (internal liquidity) has actually formed.
Misidentifying a short-term swing as external range liquidity when it is actually internal, leading to premature target selection.
Treating a sweep of external liquidity as a breakout and trading in the direction of the sweep, when price fails to displace and reverts back into the range.
Trying to play from inside the range outward during Seek and Destroy conditions instead of fading external liquidity back into equilibrium.
Confusing Relatively Equal Highs/Lows with external liquidity — external liquidity is a single prominent swing point, while equal highs/lows are multiple clustered points.
Using old swept swing highs/lows as valid External Range Liquidity targets without updating to new external levels.
Expecting price to move to External Range Liquidity before Internal Range Liquidity (Fair Value Gap) has been filled, ignoring the alternating order.
Misjudging a sweep as complete when price only touches the high/low but does not close back inside the range.
Ignoring different timeframe levels of external liquidity (weekly, daily, session highs/lows), leading to incomplete target setting.
Neglecting Internal Range Liquidity as a subsequent target after external liquidity is swept, leaving no profit target.
Mechanically expecting price to retrace to internal liquidity after every external sweep — sometimes price continues directly.

related_concepts.term: Change in the State of Delivery

merge_notes: School conflict: 2 ICT vs 1 SMC; picked ICT by majority. The Foundation card (Chinese) definition translated and integrated. Nuance preserved: special session windows from Daily Profiles, fractal application from Education ICT, and distinction from equal highs/lows from The Foundation.
